title: Fallout - The Board Game: Having Fun With a Flawed Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bHuv7kKqe-A

Daniel (@dungeondive) makes the case for a game most people dislike: Fantasy Flight's Fallout board game. He's candid about the tragic flaw — the win/lose condition can arrive out of nowhere, largely outside the player's control, the same problem he has with Red Raven's Near and Far, where you're better off ignoring the most interesting part of the game to actually win. His solution is simply to stop caring about winning and play it solo for the story.

On those terms he loves it. The genius is the deck system: two small encounter decks that evolve as you draw from a massive quest deck, sometimes building a card-map to explore a vault, sometimes seeding multi-part quest chains (he recounts educating the orphaned "Boxcar Children" while delving Vault 44). It's almost a single-session legacy game that resets fresh each play. He praises the SPECIAL leveling system — each XP nudges a letter token forward, mimicking the video game's slowing curve — and the restraint of cardboard tokens over a sea of minis. His wish: the quests deserved art and flavor, and the game might have been better with a quest-point victory than its arbitrary timer. Flawed, but with the California expansion and a discount, a rewarding solo wasteland.

----

Daniel argues Fallout nails the *feeling* of exploring a world but stumbles on how you win or lose. Are you able to enjoy a game purely for its journey, or does a broken victory condition ruin it for you?
